heading: TONUS SEPTIMUS
card_summary: This chunk opens Tonus Septimus with its first chapter, "An resurrectio sit possibilis" (Whether resurrection is possible). Zorzi frames bodily resurrection as the culmination of universal harmony: the supreme Craftsman (Opifex summus) who created the world through consonance will bring it to a perfect consummation when dissonant matter is finally laid aside. He adduces three biblical precedents for transformation of the body before death — Enoch (who walked with God and was seen no more in corruptible flesh), Elijah (taken up in a fiery chariot), and Moses (whose face shone with divine splendor on Sinai, and whose body was not left to corruption or to diabolical power). The passage cites Paul's dictum "seminatur quod animale, surgit quod spirituale" (1 Cor. 15:44) and alludes to the apocryphal dispute between the archangel Michael and the devil over Moses's body (Epistle of Jude 9), interpreted through Michael's role as prince over souls and spirits.
essay:
## Overview and Structural Position

Chunk 98 opens the seventh and final "Tonus" of *De Harmonia Mundi*, signaling that the entire cosmological-musical edifice has been building toward an eschatological resolution. The chapter heading — *An resurrectio sit possibilis* (Whether resurrection is possible) — is presented as a formal quaestio in scholastic style, though Zorzi's approach is characteristically synthetic, weaving Neoplatonic cosmology, Scripture, and patristic typology into a unified argument.

## The Cosmic Craftsman and the Final Consonance

Zorzi opens with a programmatic statement that links his cosmogony to eschatology through the governing metaphor of harmony:

> Opifex ille summus, cuius spiritus omnium artifex suavissimus est, sicuti summa consonantia in opus exivit, ipsumque cum tranquillitate disponit, et moderatur, sic tandem ad perfectum, et consummatum concentum, foeliciori et perfecta sorte deducet.

<cite>p. 2374</cite>

The *Opifex summus* (supreme Craftsman) — a Platonic-Philonic designation for God as demiurge — whose Spirit (*spiritus*) is the most gentle artificer of all things, went forth into his work in "supreme consonance" (*summa consonantia*). The final end of creation will be a "perfect and consummated harmony" (*perfectum et consummatum concentum*) achieved when man, described as the *consummatissimum Dei opus* (most perfect work of God), is fitted to his Maker in perfect modulations (*perfectis modulis*). This framing is central to Zorzi's project: resurrection is not merely a theological datum but the cosmological telos — the resolution of all dissonance into final accord.

## The Problem of Dissonant Matter

The obstacle to this consummation is matter itself. Zorzi describes it as "dissonant and horrible" (*dissonam et horridam materiam*) that by its own nature tends toward dissolution. Death is the laying aside of this dissonance:

> Illam etenim dissonam et horridam materiam morte deponimus, ut eam in meliorem sortem commutatam resumamus, et aliquando vi quadam spiritus vehementissima in melius commutemus.

<cite>p. 2374</cite>

The body that is laid down is taken up again transformed into a better condition — not annihilated but transmuted. The verb *commutare* (to transform, exchange) carries weight: resurrection is not resuscitation but alchemical transmutation, the body refined from corruptible animality into spiritual substance.

## The Three Biblical Prototypes: Enoch, Elijah, Moses

Zorzi marshals three Old Testament figures as empirical precedents for bodily transformation prior to or at death:

**Enoch**: The first case (*Enoc primo contigit*). Enoch "walked with God and was no longer seen in passible and corruptible flesh." The tradition of Enoch's translation (Gen. 5:24, elaborated in 1 Enoch and Ben Sira 44:16) demonstrates that the Spirit's power can effect bodily transformation in life.

**Elijah**: Taken up in a fiery chariot (2 Kings 2:11). The *currus igneus* (fiery chariot) figures the transforming power of divine fire, a topos also exploited in kabbalistic and Merkabah contexts, though Zorzi does not develop that dimension here.

**Moses**: The most theologically elaborate case. Moses is presented on two grounds. First, his face shone (*facies... adeo splendebat*) after divine encounter such that the children of Israel could not look upon it (Exod. 34:29–35) — Zorzi reads this as "a most evident sign of a body already transformed for the better" (*signum evidentissimum erat corporis iam in melius transmutati*). Second, Moses did not see corruption, his body was not found among human remains, and it was not left to the devil's power as other corpses are:

> non vidit corruptionem, nec inter homines reperitur, nec diaboli potestati, sicut alia cadavera, relictum est, sed transmutatum in naturam spiritus.

<cite>p. 2374–2375</cite>

## Paul's Resurrection Formula and the Michael-Devil Dispute

Zorzi anchors the Moses argument in two New Testament texts. The Pauline formula from 1 Corinthians 15:44 is cited directly: *Seminatur quod animale, surgit quod spirituale* — "it is sown an animal body, it rises a spiritual body." This provides the theological grammar for the entire sequence: the three Old Testament figures are forward-type instantiations of the eschatological transformation Paul describes.

The second citation is more unusual: the apocryphal dispute over Moses's body. Zorzi identifies this as the altercation mentioned by "Thaddaeus" (*a Thadaeo*) — i.e., the Epistle of Jude (v. 9), attributed in some traditions to Jude Thaddaeus — in which the archangel Michael contends with the devil over Moses's body. Zorzi's gloss is precise: Michael is *constitutus est princeps super omnes animas et spiritus suscipiendos* — appointed prince over all souls and spirits to be received. The dispute is thus not merely legendary but structurally meaningful: Moses's body, having been spiritually transmuted, falls under Michael's jurisdiction (souls and spirits), not the devil's (corrupting matter).

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This opening chapter of Tonus Septimus establishes that the entire harmonic cosmology of *De Harmonia Mundi* — the consonances of angelic intelligences, the musical ratios of the planetary spheres, the kabbalistic structure of the sefirot — has resurrection as its eschatological payoff. The *concentus* of the final Tonus is literally the music of the resurrection: humanity, the most perfect instrument, finally tuned perfectly to its Maker. The chapter is programmatic for whatever follows in Tonus Septimus, positioning resurrection as philosophically and cosmologically necessary, not merely dogmatically asserted.
